title: The Nuanced (and Slightly Chaotic) Reality of 'Vibe Coding'
author: Kathy Korevec
text:
The Nuanced (and Slightly Chaotic) Reality of 'Vibe Coding'
Dispatches from the frontier of vibe coding
AI coding agents like Replit Agent have made it easier than ever to build software with a few clever prompts and a dash of ambition. Want to spin up an app? Talk to your agent. Need a dashboard? Wish granted. It’s magical, until it’s not.
Amjad, Replit’s CEO, put it bluntly:
“I no longer think you should learn to code.”
He’s not saying stop thinking altogether. Quite the opposite:
“I would say learn how to think, learn how to break down problems, right? Learn how to communicate clearly, as you would with humans, but also with machines.”
I love a good thought experiment as much as the next person, and yes, thinking clearly is foundational. But let’s be real: we still need people who speak the language of software. Code isn’t just output; it’s architecture. It’s a bit like shooting with a Polaroid. You point, prompt, and something develops. Sometimes it’s magic. Sometimes it’s a blur. You’re not adjusting the aperture or fiddling with lenses, you’re relying on instinct and hoping the lighting holds. It’s fast, it’s fun, and when it works, it captures something real. Just don’t forget to learn how the camera works too. It’s how we build things that last longer than a weekend hackathon.
And here’s the part that keeps me up at night: the looming vibe-coded graveyard. A whole internet of half-built projects, broken promises, and janky prototypes. I have a few of these myself—a habit tracker, a new portfolio site, a maternity leave planner... all vibes, no follow-through. They sit quietly, haunting my v0.dev dashboard with ideas like digital dust bunnies.
Then there’s the security side. If you don’t understand what your agent is doing, how can you know if it’s safe? AI-generated code can be shockingly good. But it can also be confidently wrong. Bugs, security holes, questionable architecture; it’s all fair game when you’re coding by vibes. My biggest worry? Exposed secrets. They’re often accidentally embedded in code and swept up by bad actors, bitcoin miners, spam networks, you name it, who use them to rack up massive bills on AWS or other platforms. That’s not hypothetical; it’s already happening.
So yes, let’s teach people how to think. Let’s encourage exploration. But let’s also make sure they learn the basics of how software works. Otherwise, we’re just handing out power tools to people who’ve never seen a blueprint.
Vibe coding is fun. It’s fast. It’s the future. But if we’re not careful, it’ll also be the junk drawer of the internet. And nobody wants to debug that.
I’ve got my critiques, but I’m still all in. Vibe coding is happening, and honestly, it’s kind of electric. Natural language is becoming the abstraction layer, more human, more accessible, and that means more people get to experience the thrill of shipping something real. It feels like a superpower: taking a spark of an idea and bringing it to life in an afternoon. That’s the magic. And when it works? It really works.
I vibe coded two projects last weekend. Real ideas, real usage. One of them, Park to Wiggle, came out of an hour-long sprint on a Sunday afternoon. I had a clear itch (find a better bike route from Golden Gate Park to the Wiggle) and let the agent handle the scaffolding while I focused on describing the need clearly and iterating in the browser. No full-blown planning doc, no design phase. Just a prompt, an email API, and some tweaks. It’s scrappy, but it’s up, live, and actually helping people. That’s the real power of this kind of coding: fast loops between idea, prototype, and usage.
And with all this AI-fueled vibe coding, there’s simply going to be more software, more little tools, more weird experiments, more ideas out in the wild. That’s not noise; that’s opportunity. The pie gets bigger. More people are building, and that means more stuff to bounce off of, learn from, remix, and grow.
We don’t need to throw out the idea of coding. We need to expand what it means. Vibe coding isn’t a replacement. Vibe coding is a different mindset, a new path into the craft. The truth is more layered: natural language is a powerful new interface, but the best builders will still be the ones who understand what’s happening beneath the hood.
So dream big, ship fast, and vibe code responsibly. Nothing kills the magic faster than a leaky app and an overdue AWS bill.
But if you lean into it, with just enough structure to hold the chaos, you might surprise yourself. You might build something that helps someone. Something that sticks.
Because the real threat isn’t vibe coding, it’s vibe debugging. And the antidote to that is fluency. Not just in syntax, but in the language of software: how systems work, how they fail, and how to think clearly inside complexity. We need to level it up. Vibe coding lowers the floor. The language of software still raises the ceiling.
